summary:

title: R.H. Drexel: Along the High Embankments: Deepening the Wine Culture of Santa Barbara Wine Country
summary:
“I wanted to see…a lot of my painting deal with the ocean, the sky, the horizon, and environmental policy, and why not take that into the physical realm and address the subjects literally through staring at the horizon, exposing works to the saltwater and rain, feeling the motion of a ship, dealing with sea sickness. I’m curious if they remain calm or perhaps, they turn a different direction and get a little more chaotic themselves, searching for what feels more like home.” Cole Sternberg                        the nature of breathing in salt                        A predictable Pacific chill has descended upon the Santa Maria Valley this blustery June evening as I make my way inside a large Brutalist-like concrete bunker just off East Clark Avenue, not far from California highway 101. A once-abandoned underground barrel room, this imposing structure is the new home of Presqu’ile x LAND, a visionary, sweeping artistic collaboration between the Murphy family of Santa Maria Valley and the Los Angeles Nomadic Division.  The fresh partnership signals a deepening of wine culture, not only for this sub-appellation, but for all of Santa Barbara County. The culture of a winegrowing region isn’t just about the wines born and raised there, or the wineries and vineyards that populate its landscape, for that matter, or even the often-big personalities who lead the charge in building its reputation. Wine culture, that ineffable quality... Read More

The post R.H. Drexel: Along the High Embankments: Deepening the Wine Culture of Santa Barbara Wine Country appeared first on JebDunnuck.com.

title: Jeb Dunnuck: Up From The Cellar 30
summary:
This latest Up From The Cellar is another eclectic mix! First and foremost, it includes a number of Northern Rhônes that I missed getting into the regional report earlier this year (full report). Producers included here are François Villard, Yves Cuilleron, Christophe Pichon, and Clusel-Roch. A heartfelt apology for missing those wines earlier. Beyond that, we have a number of new releases from California, as well as the full list of 2024s from Carter. There’s a small retrospective from Quintessa as well. The report is heavy on older Bordeaux, with some real gems that I was thrilled to taste. There are some First Growths from legendary years, as well as a mix of other terrific châteaux with some real age. This report includes the latest releases from Château de la Négly. These will fall to my colleague Andy Chabot going forward, but I was thrilled to be able to taste them. The lineup is packed with rich, textured, yet beautifully balanced wines. I also was able to sit down with Antoine Veiry of Château Montus and certainly came away impressed. The whites are brilliant as well as ageworthy, and I think the reds here bring lots of power and structure, with the newer releases showing another level of elegance. These are terrific wines. There’s also a handful of Burgundies. I purchased a set of the 2019s from Domaine Rossignol-Trapet and the wines showed beautifully. And last but not least, there are a handful of beautiful Southern Rhônes. As always, I... Read More

The post Jeb Dunnuck: Up From The Cellar 30 appeared first on JebDunnuck.com.

title: Importer Highlight: Hand Picked Selections
summary:
My ongoing coverage of the small, artisanal importers continues with this latest report on Hand Picked Selections, now under the ownership of Sunil Khanna and business partner Chris Desor. Sunil worked alongside founder Dan Kravitz for some 14 years before taking the reins, and the portfolio remains exactly what longtime followers have come to expect: a deep well of family-owned growers, small estates, and top co-ops sourced from across Europe and beyond. This latest tasting confirms the quality and value are very much intact, with a wide range of wines spanning the Languedoc, Roussillon, Savoie, Burgundy, Chablis, Sancerre, and Provence, with a mix of terrific summer quaffers as well as serious wines for the cellar. These wines were tasted at my office in Colorado, and as always with this portfolio, there’s plenty here to satisfy both the palate and the pocketbook.

The post Importer Highlight: Hand Picked Selections appeared first on JebDunnuck.com.

title: JebDunnuck.com Broadens Global Coverage with the Addition of Andy Chabot and Kate Janecek
summary:
Leading fine wine publication strengthens its European editorial expertise with expanded regional coverage across France.  Boulder, CO – June 11, 2026 — Noted wine critic Jeb Dunnuck, founder of JebDunnuck.com, announced the addition of two highly respected wine professionals to its growing team of contributors and critics: Andy Chabot, who will cover the wines of Languedoc and Roussillon, and Kate Janecek, who will focus on Chablis and the Loire Valley. These additions reflect JebDunnuck.com’s continued commitment to delivering in-depth, regionally focused coverage of the world’s most dynamic wine regions, featuring experienced voices with deep industry knowledge and on-the-ground expertise. “I’ve long admired the way Andy and Kate approach wine. Both have extensive tasting experience, deep knowledge of their regions, and the kind of honest, unvarnished perspective our readers value,” said Jeb Dunnuck, Founder and Publisher of JebDunnuck.com. “As collectors continue to discover the depth and value these regions offer, I’m excited to bring two professionals of their caliber onto the team.” Andy Chabot – Languedoc and Roussillon Andy Chabot joins JebDunnuck.com as the publication’s critic covering the Languedoc and Roussillon. Chabot began his career in the service side of hospitality, studying at the Culinary Institute of America before refining his palate and career through roles at some of the country’s most respected wine programs, including Blackberry Farm in Tennessee, where he continues to play an active leadership role. At Blackberry Farm and Blackberry Mountain, he serves as Senior Vice President of Food and Beverage, overseeing hospitality strategy, wine programming, and... Read More

The post JebDunnuck.com Broadens Global Coverage with the Addition of Andy Chabot and Kate Janecek appeared first on JebDunnuck.com.

title: R.H. Drexel: Ones to Watch: Paso Robles
summary:
Breathe, breathe in the air Set your intentions Dream with care Tomorrow is a new day for everyone, Brand new moon, brand new sun. Xavier Rudd, Follow the Sun This year marks my 40th in the wine business. Even after all these years, I still love to hop in my car when I’ve got a day off and go wine tasting. Usually, I like to visit two wineries before lunch, and then two afterwards.  Every wine region worth its salt keeps evolving. In my entire career, I’ve never had the occasion to say that I won’t be returning to a wine region because it’s always the same. New wine producers emerge every day, and established ones often reinvent themselves with new projects. Just when you think you know a region, you’ll return to discover new tasting rooms, new plantings, and winery buildings popping up here and there. Here are a handful of cool, Paso Robles-based projects that have captured my attention of late. They’re being given life by talented, hardworking humans, all wishing to add their own voice to the cultural landscape of the Central Coast. Cecelia Aguirre at work in the cellar Loom Wines: Founded in 2021 by husband and wife team Diego and Cecilia Aguirre, Loom is a house devoted to Rhône-style wines. The name “Loom” takes its inspiration from the looms of Oaxaca, the couple’s ancestral homeland, known for producing vibrant rugs and other textiles. “Mother Nature, our babies, winemaking…these are threads that weave our life together,... Read More

The post R.H. Drexel: Ones to Watch: Paso Robles appeared first on JebDunnuck.com.

title: Jeb Dunnuck: Up From The Cellar 30
summary:
This latest Up From The Cellar is another eclectic mix! First and foremost, it includes a number of Northern Rhônes that I missed getting into the regional report earlier this year (full report). Producers included here are François Villard, Yves Cuilleron, Christophe Pichon, and Clusel-Roch. A heartfelt apology for missing those wines earlier. Beyond that, we have a number of new releases from California, as well as the full list of 2024s from Carter. There’s a small retrospective from Quintessa as well. The report is heavy on older Bordeaux, with some real gems that I was thrilled to taste. There are some First Growths from legendary years, as well as a mix of other terrific châteaux with some real age. This report includes the latest releases from Château de la Négly. These will fall to my colleague Andy Chabot going forward, but I was thrilled to be able to taste them. The lineup is packed with rich, textured, yet beautifully balanced wines. I also was able to sit down with Antoine Veiry of Château Montus and certainly came away impressed. The whites are brilliant as well as ageworthy, and I think the reds here bring lots of power and structure, with the newer releases showing another level of elegance. These are terrific wines. There’s also a handful of Burgundies. I purchased a set of the 2019s from Domaine Rossignol-Trapet and the wines showed beautifully. And last but not least, there are a handful of beautiful Southern Rhônes. As always, I... Read More

My ongoing coverage of the small, artisanal importers continues with this latest report on Hand Picked Selections, now under the ownership of Sunil Khanna and business partner Chris Desor. Sunil worked alongside founder Dan Kravitz for some 14 years before taking the reins, and the portfolio remains exactly what longtime followers have come to expect: a deep well of family-owned growers, small estates, and top co-ops sourced from across Europe and beyond. This latest tasting confirms the quality and value are very much intact, with a wide range of wines spanning the Languedoc, Roussillon, Savoie, Burgundy, Chablis, Sancerre, and Provence, with a mix of terrific summer quaffers as well as serious wines for the cellar. These wines were tasted at my office in Colorado, and as always with this portfolio, there’s plenty here to satisfy both the palate and the pocketbook.

content:
Leading fine wine publication strengthens its European editorial expertise with expanded regional coverage across France.  Boulder, CO – June 11, 2026 — Noted wine critic Jeb Dunnuck, founder of JebDunnuck.com, announced the addition of two highly respected wine professionals to its growing team of contributors and critics: Andy Chabot, who will cover the wines of Languedoc and Roussillon, and Kate Janecek, who will focus on Chablis and the Loire Valley. These additions reflect JebDunnuck.com’s continued commitment to delivering in-depth, regionally focused coverage of the world’s most dynamic wine regions, featuring experienced voices with deep industry knowledge and on-the-ground expertise. “I’ve long admired the way Andy and Kate approach wine. Both have extensive tasting experience, deep knowledge of their regions, and the kind of honest, unvarnished perspective our readers value,” said Jeb Dunnuck, Founder and Publisher of JebDunnuck.com. “As collectors continue to discover the depth and value these regions offer, I’m excited to bring two professionals of their caliber onto the team.” Andy Chabot – Languedoc and Roussillon Andy Chabot joins JebDunnuck.com as the publication’s critic covering the Languedoc and Roussillon. Chabot began his career in the service side of hospitality, studying at the Culinary Institute of America before refining his palate and career through roles at some of the country’s most respected wine programs, including Blackberry Farm in Tennessee, where he continues to play an active leadership role. At Blackberry Farm and Blackberry Mountain, he serves as Senior Vice President of Food and Beverage, overseeing hospitality strategy, wine programming, and... Read More

The post JebDunnuck.com Broadens Global Coverage with the Addition of Andy Chabot and Kate Janecek appeared first on JebDunnuck.com.

title: R.H. Drexel: Ones to Watch: Paso Robles
summary:
Breathe, breathe in the air Set your intentions Dream with care Tomorrow is a new day for everyone, Brand new moon, brand new sun. Xavier Rudd, Follow the Sun This year marks my 40th in the wine business. Even after all these years, I still love to hop in my car when I’ve got a day off and go wine tasting. Usually, I like to visit two wineries before lunch, and then two afterwards.  Every wine region worth its salt keeps evolving. In my entire career, I’ve never had the occasion to say that I won’t be returning to a wine region because it’s always the same. New wine producers emerge every day, and established ones often reinvent themselves with new projects. Just when you think you know a region, you’ll return to discover new tasting rooms, new plantings, and winery buildings popping up here and there. Here are a handful of cool, Paso Robles-based projects that have captured my attention of late. They’re being given life by talented, hardworking humans, all wishing to add their own voice to the cultural landscape of the Central Coast. Cecelia Aguirre at work in the cellar Loom Wines: Founded in 2021 by husband and wife team Diego and Cecilia Aguirre, Loom is a house devoted to Rhône-style wines. The name “Loom” takes its inspiration from the looms of Oaxaca, the couple’s ancestral homeland, known for producing vibrant rugs and other textiles. “Mother Nature, our babies, winemaking…these are threads that weave our life together,... Read More
